column602: Six-domain definition of MCS syndrome: The 6-domain definition of the condition required the weekly reporting of at least 1 of 8 neurobehavioral symptoms, at least 1 of 3 symptoms related to oder tolerance (hypersensitivity or hyposensitivity), and at least 1 symptom from 2 other organ systems (collectively constituting domain 1). For domain 2, an individual had to score 80 of 126 or higher on scales that measured several common environmental chemical exposures (defined roughly in terms of parameters related to dose and duration of exposure), and some degree of illness had to have been produced. For domain 3, there had to have been at least 2 changed tolerances to environmental stimuli--1 for chemical odors and the other for 1 of the following: food, allergens, medications, alcohol, and sunlight. For domain 4, recovery time from illness following chemical exposure had to have exceeded 12 hrs. For domains 5 and 6, awareness of chemical odors and difficulty ignoring them, and attribution of illness to chemical exposures at least once during the past month, respectively, were the defining features. We used receiver operator curves to select the cut points for domains 2 and 4 to maximize agreement between these domains and scores based on domains 1, 3, 5, and 6.

Self-report of physician-diagnosed MCS (primary survey) and QEESI (secondary survey)

The QEESI consists of 50 questions divided into five sections: I) chemical exposures, II) other exposures, III) symptoms, IV) masking index, and V) impact of sensi- tivities. All sections except IV) (masking index) are scored with a total of 0 to 100 points. MCS risk is based on chemical exposures (section I) and symptoms (sec- tion III). Individuals with a total score ≥ 40 in each of these sections are defined as ‘very suggestive of MCS’ [11], whereas those with a total score < 40 are defined as ‘not suggestive of MCS’

Cullen criteria:
1. The disorder is acquired in concert with some documentable
2. Symptoms involve more than one organ system
3. Symptoms recur and abate in response to predictable stimuli
4. Symptoms are elicited by exposures to chemicals of diverse structural classes and toxicologic modes of action
5. Symptoms are elicited by exposures that are demonstrable (albeit low level)

7. No single widely available test of organ system function can explain symptoms

According to QEESI interpretation results, participants were divided into 4 groups: not suggestive (symptom severity score <40 and chemical intolerance score <40; symptom severity score >=40, chemical score <40, and masking score <4), problematic group (symptom severity score <40 and chemical intolerance score >=40), somewhat suggestive group (symptom severity score >=40, chemical intolerance score <40, and masking score >=4), and very suggestive group (symptom severity score >=40 and chemical intolerance score >=40). column689: The results indicate that the irritative potential of 2-ethylhexanol is higher than commonly expected. In both exposure scenarios with either constant or peak exposures, electromyographic eye blink recordings were an appropriate method for the examination of acute sensory irritations in time. The results confirmed a general effect of selfreported MCS on subjective estimations of acute symptoms. Independently from the current exposure condition the time courses of sensory irritations were affected by sMCS. There might be a cognitive bias for the increase of sensory irritations of sMCS-subjects due to slightly stronger perceptional cross talk between olfactory and trigeminal symptoms as indicated by the slightly different associations.{#1074} The hypothesis of stronger responses of autonomic functions of sMCS-subjects provoked by various exposure scenarios remains open.{#6613} While the assumption of a generally altered sensitivity of autonomic functions of sMCS-subjects to environmental changes seems to be supported, no specific reactions to the type or level of the chemical exposure were found.{#956} Participants with sMCS had a significant decrease in the flow value in anterior rhinomanometry, independent of substance and doses, compared with controls; this suggests somatic reactions to the exposure.{#1242}

column32: Cullen Criteria: 1. The disorder is acquired in relation to a documentable environmental exposure.2. The symptoms come from more than one organ system (unlike allergic asthma which affects the lungs only).3. Symptoms come and go in response to predictable stimuli. (The symptoms are not continuous and do not predate exposure.)4. Symptoms are produced by exposure to chemicals of diverse structural classes and toxicological modes of action. (Substances to which patients with M.C.S. react are inherently toxic and are found in pesticides, petrochemicals and other solvents, formaldehyde, building materials, cigarette smoke, perfumes, medications, exhaust fumes, cooking gas, deodorizers and cleaning agents.)5. Symptoms are produced by exposures that are demonstrable even in low concentrations. (Another person not affected should verify the exposure.)6. Exposures that produce symptoms must be a standard deviation below average exposures known to produce adverse human responses, thus excluding those who are exposed to a clearly toxic level of chemical.7. No single test of organ function can explain symptoms (there are no abnormal physical findings).

Therefore, the existence of CNS symptoms should be regarded as an obligatory diagnostic criterion for the diagnosis of MCS. In addition, only those conditions should yield a diagnosis of MCS in which a significant loss of lifestyle and in functional impairment leading to a chronic state of the disease has occurred. While the case definition of the US MCS-conference  (1999)  has provided a still useful and valid basis of diagnostic criteria for MCS the results of the present review suggest to specify these criteria as follows: the predominant complaint in MCS involves a symptom of the CNS in association with self-reported odor hypersensitivity. The symptoms are lasting for at least 6 months and are associated with significant functional impairment and change in life-style. In addition at least one further functional symptom in another organ system has occurred. As none of the available studies differentiates between exposure-related and non-exposure-related complaints in detail (the latter may have resulted from other disorders), a complete and precise description of all potentially obligatory, exposure-related CNS-symptoms is not possible at present. Authors suggest that exposure-related symptoms, which are associated with self-reported MCS, should be divided into a hierarchy of criteria: symptoms of the CNS, which we consider as main characteristics or mandatory criteria and non-CNS symptoms, which we consider as optional criteria.

column32:
Cullen criteria-based:
1. The syndrome is acquired, usually after the occurance of a more clearly evident (although not necessarily serious) health event caused by environmental exposure, such as solvent intoxication, respiratory tract irritation, pesticide poisoning, or sick building syndrome
2. The patient experiences multiple symptoms referable to several organ systems, almost always including the central nervous system.
3. Although there may be persistent complaints between exposures, the symptoms are characteristically and predictably precipitated by a perceived environmental exposure.
4. The agents that may precipitate the symptoms are multiple and chemically diverse.
5. The doses of these agents that may precipitate the symptoms are at least two orders of magnitude lower than the established thresholds for acute health effects.
6. No test of physiologic function can explain the symptoms. Although there may be clinical abnormalities, such as mild bronchospasm or neuropsychologic dysfunction, these are insufficient to explain the illness pattern.
7. No other organic disorder is present that can explain the pattern of symptoms.

column42: At the request of the Environmental Protection Agency, the National Research Council conducted a workshop to develop a research agenda to study the phenomenon of MCS. The workshop convened with the overall objective of addressing gaps in the scientific evidence on MCS. Three groups were formed to develop a research agenda. The subjects assigned were 1) case evaluation and criteria for diagnosis 2) mechanism potentially underlying MCS and 3) epidemiologic approaches to investigating MCS. The first working group provided lists of case criteria, candidate populations for investigation, and approaches for evaluating patients with multiple chemical sensitivity. The group advocated the use of an environmental control unit to test the adaptation-deadaptation hypothesis. The workshop participants agreed that the recommendations of this working group should have the highest priority in any research agenda. The second group also proposed the use of controlled exposures of subjects in whom multiple chemical sensitivity is diagnosed and of control subjects. This group advocated the formulation of animal models, the investigation of tissues, and the development of a data base of chemicals, foods, and drugs reported to be associated with multiple chemical sensitivity. The third group was concerned with epidemiologic approaches. A multicenter clinical assessment of patients was advocated for early attention. The results of the multicenter study would be used to develop methods for determining the prevalence of multiple chemical sensitivity. This group also suggested follow-up of populations with discrete and sudden chemical exposures.

column32: Yes to the question "Do you experience any unpleasant symptoms when exposued to certain chemical substances with distinctive odous (e.g., gases, cleaning products), even at a dosage that is generally well tolerated by most people?" and answers rarely, often, or every time when asked "How often do you experience symptoms in such cases?" and indicates some, medium, or high impact when asked "On the whole to what extent do the symptoms (if they appear) affect our everyday life (.e.g., work leisure, traveling)?"
column81: Reddington K, Witthöft M, Köteles F, Szemerszky R. Symptoms associated with environmental factors are positively related to sensory-processing sensitivity. J Psychosom Res. 2025. 199:112425
column602: Rated above average perceived sensitivity when exposed to certain chemical substances with distinctive odors, at least some regularity in the reaction to the chemicals, and at least some impact on everyday functioning
column689: Sensory-processing sensitivity (SPS) can be considered a newly described (although not independent) psychological concomitant of symptoms associated with environmental factors (SAEFs). Understanding the background of the association between somatosensory amplification and SPS can be helpful to better understand SAEFs.
